era_context: The Akkadian Empire has fully collapsed; Gutian warlords control Mesopotamia. Egypt is in the First Intermediate Period, with power fragmented between Memphis-based dynasties and Upper Egyptian nomarchs. The Indus Valley Civilization continues at its mature phase. In China, the Xia Dynasty (if historical) persists in its early-to-middle period. Climate stress from the 4.2 kya event may still be affecting agricultural production in the Near East and Egypt.

events.title: Lagash under Lugal-anda consolidates power; inter-city conflicts in Sumer
events.description: Lugal-anda, ruler of the city-state of Lagash, consolidates power and expands Lagash's influence over neighbouring territories. This period sees increased inter-city warfare among Sumerian city-states as they compete for resources and prestige in the absence of a unified Mesopotamian empire. Lagash's inscriptions record military campaigns and administrative reforms. The Gutian overlords remain in the background, exerting nominal control but not directly governing individual city-states.
key_figures: Lugal-anda (ruler of Lagash, traditional dates c. 2180–2170 BCE)
events.certainty_note: Lugal-anda is attested in contemporary inscriptions from Lagash. However, the precise dating of his reign within the Gutian period remains uncertain; traditional dates are c. 2180–2170 BCE, but this may be off by ±10–20 years.
cross_references: Part of the Gutian interregnum period
Precedes the rise of Gudea of Lagash (c. 2144–2124 BCE)

events.title: Upper Egyptian nomarchs consolidate regional power; Heracleopolitan Dynasty (Ninth Dynasty) emerges in Lower Egypt
events.description: During the First Intermediate Period, the Ninth Dynasty (Heracleopolitan Dynasty) emerges as a power in Lower Egypt, based at Herakleopolis Magna. This dynasty claims pharaonic authority but controls only a limited territory. Meanwhile, in Upper Egypt, powerful nomarchs continue to rule as independent princes, with the Eleventh Dynasty (based at Thebes) gradually rising in prominence. The period is characterized by political fragmentation and the absence of a unified Egyptian state. Contemporary sources include nomarchal tomb inscriptions and administrative records.
key_figures: Kheti I (Ninth Dynasty pharaoh, traditional dates c. 2160–2140 BCE)
Upper Egyptian nomarchs (names recorded in tomb inscriptions)

sources.name: Nomarchal tomb inscriptions from Upper Egypt

events.certainty_note: The emergence of the Ninth Dynasty and the rise of the Eleventh Dynasty are attested in contemporary sources and later king lists. However, Egyptian chronology for the First Intermediate Period has a margin of error of ±20–30 years.
cross_references: Part of the First Intermediate Period (-2181 to -2055 BCE)
Precedes the Middle Kingdom unification (c. -2055 BCE)

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization: continued urban prosperity and long-distance trade
events.region: Indus Valley (modern Pakistan and northwest India)
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continues to flourish, with major urban centres maintaining their administrative and commercial functions. Trade networks extend to Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, and Central Asia. Standardized weights and measures facilitate commerce. No specific events are attested for this year, but the civilization's economic and administrative systems remain robust. The gradual decline that will characterize the late Indus period (c. 1900–1300 BCE) has not yet begun.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, Dholavira, and other Indus sites

events.certainty_note: The flourishing of the Indus Valley Civilization during this period is confirmed by extensive archaeological evidence. However, the absence of deciphered written records means that specific events or chronological precision for individual years cannot be established.
cross_references: Contemporary with Mesopotamian and Egyptian political fragmentation
Part of the mature Indus Valley Civilization phase

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of Lugal-anda's reign (c. 2180–2170 BCE) is based on the Sumerian King List, which is considered unreliable for chronological precision. Some scholars argue that the Gutian period was shorter than traditionally believed, which would shift the dating of rulers like Lugal-anda. The Ninth Dynasty's emergence and its relationship to the Eighth Dynasty remain debated; some scholars question whether the Ninth Dynasty was truly a separate dynasty or merely a continuation of the Eighth Dynasty under a different name.
historiographic_note: Sources for this year remain sparse and fragmentary. Egyptian sources are more abundant than Mesopotamian sources, but both are biased toward elite perspectives. The Indus Valley Civilization's undeciphered script continues to limit our understanding. Paleoclimatic evidence suggests that climate stress may still be affecting agricultural production, but the specific impact on individual societies is difficult to quantify. The absence of written records from most of the world means that the histories of Africa, the Americas, and most of Asia and Europe during this period are reconstructed from archaeology alone, with large gaps.

graph_edges.note: Lugal-anda's consolidation of power in Lagash occurs during the same period as the emergence of the Heracleopolitan Dynasty in Egypt
Lugal-anda's consolidation of power in Lagash precedes the rise of Gudea, who will become the most famous ruler of Lagash
The fragmentation of Egypt during the First Intermediate Period will eventually lead to the reunification under the Middle Kingdom
